sentences.text: Yeah.
And he was gesturing, moving around.
And he-- he went-- he went away.
But in the background, we knew that this is already trouble.

There was already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a contingent-- contingent of Lithuanian, West Russian-- or Belarus-- in addition to-- especially the worst of them all, the Ukrainian, and even the even [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Hungarian-- Hungarian.
What they came-- what they-- we notice-- what we were told-- came in town.
And this was a clear sign that as well there will be something.
There will be, probably, destruction.
And sure enough, the night before-- the night before the destruction, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under darkness, under curfew, and darkness of night, we left the ghetto.
We crossed-- we crossed-- because it's by truck-- that the area what divided us was a truck.
And we went to the Pole.
But our-- our uncle, what my uncle had told us before-- his name was [?
Bernislav ?] [?
Ronalski. ?] All three of us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the middle of the night.
Well, we knocked on the door.
He came out scared.
But they put us in the-- in the--
In barn, together with the animals.
The night was extremely cold.
It was so-- I thought that I will never make the night.
The-- the swine and the cows were almost stepping on us with-- with their, and not only this, but almost making excrements.
We were all covered with excreta.
In the morning, the contingent-- the darkness, almost at night, twilight, they surrounded the ghetto.
And they took all of the Jews from the ghetto, brought them to the [GERMAN] or the collection place.
We know what's there.
There's groups of 20, 50.
They took them in-- in freshly digged graves near the corner of the woods.
And group by group, they shoot them.
[INAUDIBLE] from about 18, 19,000 people.
Only 5,000-- a thousand five hundred remained.
The rest of them were killed.
And the 1,500 people they let go back homes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Under darkness of night, we returned to the-- to the our house, but our house was already robbed.
Everything was almost in pieces.
We are were remain.
Lida is almost no more.
This is the main thing here.
The question was what to do next.
So we found out that, about at that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a truck from [GERMAN].
This is [?
Heronbaffin ?] where [?
Heronbaffin ?] came in to Lida.
Heronbaffin, ?] it's a [GERMAN].
Or Air Force, was connecting in different areas the remain of the-- of the motors from the airplanes, from the tanks, et cetera.
And some of them ammunition, bombs.
And they used to travel all around the area to connect them to take them to the front.
They needed some people.
I volunteered.
And I met up with some Jewish-- Jewish people, and the chief of the brigade, [?
Pinska ?].
Anyway, not telling anybody, just as they had after [INAUDIBLE] it's old equipment, I hide myself unver the-- the airplane motor, and decided to go back [INAUDIBLE].
Maybe back to [?
Vinno ?].
Because I heard that they;re going-- going back to [?
Vinno ?].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So the thing is, first of all, we went back to Belarus, and back to [INAUDIBLE].
Over there, they disconnected the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the truck-- the connection to the truck.
And they wheeled a [? real ?] truck went with [?
Pinska ?], with his-- he was his group-- with a German to-- into the-- into the village, a hamlet, to buy some stuff for the ghetto, because they were making business with the German.
And I remained in the-- under-- under the-- The motor.
The motor, the round motor, covered and waiting what to do.
Suddenly, I see four policemen-- Belarus-- lifting up-- lifting up on-- on the-- on the attachment to the-- to-- to the truck.
They looked at me-- and they were sure that nobody will be over there.
They could grab over there to take whatever it is.
This is the way they were-- they were grabbing everything.
But they saw me, and suddenly, they-- they looked around, and they didn't know that I'm-- maybe that I-- they left me over there to watch it.
They moved back, and went away.
This way, for six hours I remained by myself over there.
[INAUDIBLE].
And they came back.
They-- the German truck [INAUDIBLE] was a-- was a-- it was a [INAUDIBLE] where the guys from [INAUDIBLE] came in.
And they connected back the-- the attachment to the main truck.
And we went to Vilna.
And now I'm back in Vilna.
I-- I-- crew going in, just as we went in into-- into the main-- main area from [?
Bezanuska ?], what was completely surrounded by-- by wire.
I came out from my hiding place, and I saw the guys are talking English.
And I told them-- I told them I'm back from-- from Lida.
And they said, OK, we'll take you-- we'll take you in into the ghetto.
And being in the ghetto, [?
Pinksa ?] took me to the Gens.
He told me going into to in here.
And when I met Gens, first of all, he asked me if I want to join the police.
The head of the Judenrat.
This was middle.
Middle of '42.
Summer already.
And I told him that I don't want it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Not I don't want it.
I told him that I came in with [?
Pinskanufka, ?] and I would like to join the [?
Pinskanufka ?].
And he was not so happy.
He needed, at that time, policeman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did not feel up to do the-- the dirty work for him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And luckily that I did not.
Now the thing is, the other-- he was-- he was asking me, interrogating me what happened in Lida.
He wanted just to know what happened.
I did not tell him anything.
I felt that that is trouble.
Why?
Because I would like to bring a little story, what happened before, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In-- on the height of the action, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a-- a girl.
Their family was taken to Ponary.
And in the Ponary they killed everybody-- all her family.
And she fell on the dead bodies.
And she was covered with blood.
And after that, they put lime on her, and she almost got killed only from the-- from the pressure of the bodies.
Luckily, she survived.
And [INAUDIBLE] the-- the Lithuanians were so drunk-- it was mostly Lithuanians.
They were so drunk that they didn't know what was going on.
Under the darkness of-- of night, she went out from the Ponary.
Ponary was area of woods-- woods.
I have some pictures in here.
And she escaped.
And she came in.
This is a story how she even escaped.
How she went in over there to-- to one of the Christian houses.
And she asked for-- for a piece of bread, for a little water because she was so bloody in here.
And instead of-- of accepting her, accepting her, they threw her out [NON-ENGLISH].
But she give her a piece of bread.
Anyway, she came back in ghetto.
And she came in-- in the ghetto, she was living next door to us-- [NON-ENGLISH].
And the thing is, and over there, she was with an uncle.
And the uncle hide her.
And he said, keep your mouth shut.
Don't talk.
Don't tell anybody, because, first of all, it was not safe, because the gestapo can find out.
The second thing, he did not want her to make any more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emotional tension, fear-- to spread fear in the ghetto.
[INAUDIBLE].
Maybe he was scared for the administration to spread rumors.
She comes to my brother and tells him about why the guy, why not to tell.
And my brother said, the only solution is go outside if you want to.
You look like a shiksa.
She was going-- she was going to school with him.
She said, it's impossible.
They're all surrounding.
It's all hostile.
That they don't-- they recognize you if you are Jewish.
And she by herself-- I wrote her the story.
I said, why didn't they believe me?
And this is a eternal question.
They believed, but they did not want to believe.
You were looking on the truth without seeing the truth, reality.
And this is why you did not want to tell Gens what you have seen?
This was one of the problem.
Because if I would tell them too much, I was scared that I would be exposed.
And this can go a little further, and I could lose my life.
But I-- I told my friends.
--and to live in the ghetto.
Yeah.
OK, now now we're starting a different type of philosophy [INAUDIBLE].
The ghetto is transforming.
It remains only 15,000 people.
The ghetto became-- became [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] matured.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The philosophy to go as sheep to slaughter, changes in the philosophy of partial resistance.
They used what was really [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] politically oriented.
It's starting to-- I'm sorry-- organize, and to produce [? some cells. ?] And we're gradually--
Cells.
Yeah.
Resistance cells.
And by thinking about doing some-- some even-- even to send up against the German.
And now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we have-- we are starting to think about getting out.
But the outside world is numb and dumb.
It does not listen to us.
There's a lot of hate from-- from-- from the surrounding.
I did.
By going in here, first of all, by organizing-- we are probably, Vlina was the first organized resistance.
Vlina was sending out [NON-ENGLISH].
This means-- some--
--emissaries to Warsaw, to to gather stock.
This is the resistance in here.
Vlina at that time, has such great people.
Like [?
Gleuck ?] is producing his partisan, and--
Yeah.
You have [NON-ENGLISH].
You have [?
Abakovna. ?] You have Wittenberg.
You have you have-- the young [NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, sure.
I was-- I was part of them.
OK--
In the ghetto.
So the thing is, gradually-- gradually, it was developed in here the idea of [NON-ENGLISH]-- we don't have a choice.
And when you're-- and maturing.
The only thing is we do not have the experience and the-- and the preparation.
We already developed the idea.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The idea is that [REACHING FOR PAPER] the-- I would like to in here to bring in a an impression-- an expression from Filip Friedman, that-- the historian.
And it developed the idea not about if we need a resistance, but where to have the resistance-- to have the resistance in the ghetto, or to have the resistance outside the ghetto and go to the woods.
It was two schools.
One school was [?
Yarl ?] [?
Schoenbaum. ?] And the other one was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the other one was Wittenberg.
Wittenberg wanted-- was a communist.
He wanted to organize the resistance in the ghetto.
Schoenbaum ?] wanted to organize the resistance in the in outside.
To go out.
But the fate turned out just reverse.
This is now-- I'm going now to 1943.
I wanted to go out.
I wanted to-- I wanted just to [INAUDIBLE] go to the woods.
And what happened?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We knew already.
We started to hear what is going on in Warsaw.
We knew what a heroic stand, what Warsaw did.
[NON-ENGLISH] I have some people what are coming in.
And the problem was now, we knew that the German would not let us go.
In here they're preparing.
If they're going to liquidate the ghetto, they're going to liquidate and be very careful, because they knew-- they know for sure that we have already a resistance.
We are starting, but we have very little arms.
We had so little arms.
So finally, in 1943, the-- the game changes.
The-- the Germans are starting to get one defeat after the other.
And we knew, or they knew by themselves, that the time for-- for the Final Solution is almost-- almost-- [INAUDIBLE] the time of their heroic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] conquest is over.
So finally, they wanted only one
to fulfill the war against the Jews-- to finish up the war of the Jews.
And so it's a [INAUDIBLE] that there will not be a letdown.
So the thing is, we had to organize and to do the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- to organize the resistance.
And now we have September 1st, 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They are closing up the ghetto.
No more outside.
Up till now, you could go out working, and you could work, and to-- and to have-- to-- to be able to bring in food.
Now, nobody can go out.
The ghetto is on hunger-- hunger.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it looked like this is the final chapter as written.
But I would like to bring in just a few days before it.
And this is an incident what I would like to present in memory.
And the name that I would call this is hunger in the ghetto.
The ghetto started to starve.
No food-- no food is brought in.
In order to survive for us-- for-- for me, my brother, and my cousin-- we had to devise a way of bringing in the food.
We called this [NON-ENGLISH].
So the thing is, we had a special uniform that was made when my-- when my aunt was still alive, which consisted of-- of a garment.
It was division.
And over there you used to put the food in.
Like pockets.
Pockets.
And you put them on your body.
So I was going out, and we had already our special places where we used to go and to buy the food.
We went out-- once upon a time, I went out.
And this was almost on the end.
And suddenly, I bought my food, and we had a place where, instead of going through the gate, I had-- we made an opening on [?
Litksa. ?] And through the opening, we used to bring in the food, all-- all our supplies.
And after that, go back through the gate, and going in without nobody noticing.
I did.
So I-- I took-- I took off my special garment.
I put it through.
And I go back.
And suddenly, I see, under the twilight, a German-- a gendarme.
He looks at me.
He holds the revolver in-- in his hand.
And he screams, halt!
Halt!
I stop.
Comes in with the revolver, and just threw me on-- in here on the floor.
Step with his-- with his boot on my body.
And he said, [GERMAN], you [INAUDIBLE], you [INAUDIBLE].
It's hard to explain how to translate.
What-- what are you doing?
How did you do?
Where was?
How did you came out from the ghetto?
What are you doing in here?
I said, I'm going to work.
And his uniform with his eyes, he was ready to kill me.
I saw such an anger.
And turned around, looked around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, [GERMAN].
And let me go.
Shooting in [? the ?] [? air. ?] And I started to run back.
This was-- it was another thing.
Anyway, it looked like my star was still following me.
A lucky star.
Everything is-- is always luck.
Anyway, it's [? 19th ?] [? in ?] September.
And the ghetto is closed.
And we know that the final chapter is already written.
The ghetto-- getto Warsaw is already liquidated.
And the-- the thing is, they are going to-- they are going already to liquidate the [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto.
And the problem was how much longer?
